posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I managed to get to Rayas last week, as a post Bordeaux En-primeur therapy visit, my first tasting without taking notes of the year. For people who love the wines but never got to visit, these pictures might help to give a sense of the place… The tasting is great, of course, especially to taste the components of Rayas itself, which helps make sense of the whole; but the real excitement is walking the vineyard.


Rayas sign by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr


Rayas 1 by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr


WK at Rayas by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr


Rayas 2 by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr


Rayas pipette by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr


Rayas entrée by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr


Rayas panorama by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr

Brodie

Not in the days of google maps, Waze etc… and signposting has improved now that the tree that had grown so big it was hiding the sign has died!

Wow, thanks William. is it true that it is very hard to find?

Yes, based on my visit in the 80s, before Google maps. As I recall, it was hidden, some distance from the road, over a little rise. But my memory is faint at this point.

I love how Bordeaux en premieur is going!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Any pictures of the barrels? I’ve read somewhere that the old barrels are thickly coated with tartrates.

I don’t think Emmanuel likes people to take pictures inside the cellar, so I didn’t try. But, the tartrates are on the insides of the barrels, not the exterior, so you don’t see them. The cellar is full of old demi-muids which are refilled with new wine as soon as they are emptied.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
DSC04517.jpg

A Pic I took ablut 15 years ago.
One of the most remarkable things about about the property is how much of it is just woods, not vineyard land.
It gives it quite a pastoral, removed-from-the-world feel.

I think this is indeed one of the key insights into understanding the singular style of Rayas. When you drive around CdP, there is very little woodland today, and plenty of vineyards which were clearly created by ripping up woodland/scrub and restructuring the soils mechanically (any obviously mechanically crushed, jagged limestone is an indicator of this). By removing so much woodland, an already warm AOC became warmer - plus a lot of vineyards were planted on sites that were historically deemed unsuitable.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
To illustrate the point, here’s Rayas and the surrounding neighborhood in an aerial photograph from the 1950s vs today. Rayas & Pignan are the vineyards surrounded by trees in the center. Today, they are practically the only ones surrounded by trees. The vast majority of other woodland has been uprooted and planted with vines.


Screen Shot 2022-05-02 at 5.34.45 AM by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr

link_counts.title: Screen Shot 2022-05-02 at 5.34.45 AM | Rayas 1950s vs today | WilliamGFKelley | Flickr

posts.name: LasseK

Cheers,
The few images I’ve seen from Rayas vineyards has all looked liked your’s, so was just wondering.

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
Based on my hazy memory, I believe it was in late October, well after harvest.
The leaves browning on fruitless vines give an indication of the timing as well…
I do remember what it felt like, the quiet, the breeze…
William, that is a brilliant side by side of the vineyards some 70 years apart. Merci bien.
At times, it can feel as though things don’t change that much, that fast.
But when you can visually put into perspective just how much has changed in a little more than 100 years (the industrial revolution in general), it is (to my sensibilities, sorry if it seems histrionic) horrifying.

posts.name: Pat Esposit0
posts.cooked: William thank you for posting, and yes, very green with envy. Are these visits something that are available to those ‘in the business’ or can the average winehead schedule a visit if they made the trek there?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
William thank you for posting, and yes, very green with envy. Are these visits something that are available to those ‘in the business’ or can the average winehead schedule a visit if they made the trek there?

I visited twice as a student, so yes, it’s not just for pros! But you’ll need to speak French and write a letter.
